explicit_text: I was born in Fresno, California in 1991. It's where my story began before my family moved when I was young. Although I didn't grow up there, Fresno is an important part of my origin and identity. It marks the starting point of my journey as a Hmong American.
implicit_text: I was born in a Central California city in 1991. It's where my story began before my family moved when I was young. Although I didn't grow up there, that city is an important part of my origin and identity. It marks the starting point of my journey as a Hmong American.

explicit_text: My family moved to St. Paul, Minnesota in 1996, and that's where I grew up. St. Paul shaped much of my childhood and identity as a Hmong American. It’s also where I experienced many of the challenges and questions about who I am, especially growing up in a place with a diverse but sometimes unfamiliar population. This city holds many memories of my youth and personal growth.
implicit_text: My family moved to the capital city of Minnesota in 1996, and that's where I grew up. This city shaped much of my childhood and identity as a Hmong American. It’s also where I experienced many of the challenges and questions about who I am, especially growing up in a place with a diverse but sometimes unfamiliar population. This city holds many memories of my youth and personal growth.

explicit_text: I graduated from Bethel University in 2014 with a degree in Reconciliation. Attending Bethel was a significant chapter in my life, helping me deepen my understanding of identity and community. It was during and after my time there that I began to express my story more openly through poetry and public performances. Bethel provided me with the education and environment to explore my heritage and personal growth.
implicit_text: I graduated from a private Christian university in Minnesota in 2014 with a degree in Reconciliation. Attending that university was a significant chapter in my life, helping me deepen my understanding of identity and community. It was during and after my time there that I began to express my story more openly through poetry and public performances. The school provided me with the education and environment to explore my heritage and personal growth.

explicit_text: When I was a junior in high school, I went on a mission trip to New Orleans with my youth group. It was there that a memorable encounter happened—a lady greeted me with 'Aloha!' which surprised me because I had never met her before. That moment stayed with me and inspired me to write my poem 'My Name is Hmong: Call Me Freedom.' New Orleans was the backdrop for a turning point in how I viewed my identity and culture.
implicit_text: When I was a junior in high school, I went on a mission trip to a famous Louisiana city with my youth group. It was there that a memorable encounter happened—a lady greeted me with 'Aloha!' which surprised me because I had never met her before. That moment stayed with me and inspired me to write my poem 'My Name is Hmong: Call Me Freedom.' That city was the backdrop for a turning point in how I viewed my identity and culture.

explicit_text: Two years ago, my poem 'My Name is Hmong: Call Me Freedom' was published in the Saint Paul Almanac. This was my first piece to be published, and it meant a lot to me because it shared my story and pride in being Hmong with a wider audience. The Almanac helped me connect with the local community and gave me a platform to express my cultural identity. It remains a source of inspiration for me.
